questions.q: What does the abbreviation BARC stand for?
questions.explanation: BARC stands for the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, named after physicist Homi J. Bhabha.

questions.q: BARC is named after which Indian physicist?
questions.explanation: The centre was renamed in honour of Homi J. Bhabha, the founder of India's nuclear programme, after his death in 1966.

questions.q: In which city is BARC's main campus located?
questions.explanation: BARC's principal campus is at Trombay in Mumbai, Maharashtra.

questions.q: BARC is primarily a research centre for which field?
questions.explanation: BARC is India's premier nuclear research facility, central to its atomic energy programme.

questions.q: Under which national body does BARC primarily operate?
options: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
questions.explanation: BARC functions under India's Department of Atomic Energy (DAE).

questions.q: What was BARC originally called when it was founded in 1954?
questions.explanation: It was established in 1954 as the Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay (AEET), and renamed BARC in 1967.

questions.q: Apsara, commissioned at Trombay in 1956, holds what distinction?
options: The first nuclear research reactor in Asia
questions.explanation: Apsara was the first nuclear research reactor in Asia, built at Trombay and inaugurated in 1956.

questions.q: Which type of facilities is BARC best known for housing?
questions.explanation: BARC is home to several of India's research reactors, such as Apsara, CIRUS, Dhruva and Purnima.

questions.q: BARC played a central role in which historic 1974 event?
questions.explanation: Scientists at BARC developed the device for Smiling Buddha, India's first nuclear test at Pokhran in 1974.

questions.q: In which year was the Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay (later BARC) founded?
questions.explanation: The establishment that became BARC was founded in 1954.

questions.q: In which year was the Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay formally renamed Bhabha Atomic Research Centre?
questions.explanation: It was renamed BARC in 1967, a year after Homi Bhabha's death in 1966.

questions.q: Which BARC reactor, commissioned in 1960, was a Canada-India cooperative reactor whose plutonium was linked to the 1974 test?
questions.explanation: CIRUS, a 40 MW reactor built with Canadian help and US heavy water, supplied plutonium associated with the 1974 test.

questions.q: Dhruva, BARC's largest indigenous research reactor, was commissioned in which decade?
questions.explanation: Dhruva, a 100 MW indigenously designed reactor, achieved criticality in 1985.

questions.q: What does the reactor name 'CIRUS' principally signify?
options: Combined Isotope Reactor for Uranium Studies
questions.explanation: CIRUS reflected the Canada-India reactor that used heavy water supplied by the United States.

questions.q: Apsara, India's first reactor, was famously named by whom?
questions.explanation: Homi Bhabha named the reactor Apsara, after the celestial nymphs of Indian mythology.

questions.q: Which BARC research reactor's name means 'complete' or 'full' and was a small plutonium-fuelled reactor used for fast-reactor studies?
questions.explanation: Purnima was a small plutonium-fuelled research reactor at BARC used for fast-reactor physics studies.

questions.q: BARC has championed which fuel cycle, suited to India's large reserves of a particular element?
questions.explanation: Given India's abundant thorium reserves, BARC and DAE have pursued a long-term thorium-based three-stage nuclear programme.

questions.q: The 1998 Pokhran-II nuclear tests, in which BARC was deeply involved, were conducted under which operation name?
questions.explanation: Pokhran-II in 1998 was carried out as Operation Shakti, establishing India as a declared nuclear power.

questions.q: Beyond power and weapons, BARC has developed irradiation technology widely used for which civilian purpose?
options: Food preservation and medical isotope production
Desalinating seawater by reverse osmosis only
questions.explanation: BARC applies nuclear technology to food irradiation and the production of radioisotopes for medicine and industry.

questions.q: Homi Bhabha proposed a three-stage nuclear power programme for India. What is the intended fuel of the third stage?
questions.explanation: Bhabha's three-stage plan culminates in thorium-based reactors using uranium-233 bred from thorium.

questions.q: BARC operates a training school whose graduates form a major part of India's nuclear workforce. What is it commonly called?
questions.explanation: The BARC Training School trains young scientists and engineers who go on to staff the DAE's institutions.

questions.q: Who served as the first head of the Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay, the body that became BARC?
questions.explanation: Homi Bhabha founded and first headed the establishment at Trombay that later became BARC.

questions.q: Which physicist, later a BARC director, led the team that designed the device for the 1974 Smiling Buddha test?
questions.explanation: Raja Ramanna led the team behind India's first nuclear test, Smiling Buddha, in 1974.

questions.q: KAMINI, a low-power research reactor designed jointly by BARC and IGCAR, is notable for being fuelled by which isotope tied to India's thorium fuel cycle?
questions.explanation: KAMINI is fuelled by uranium-233 bred from thorium, reflecting India's thorium fuel-cycle ambitions; it was for years the only operating U-233 reactor in the world.

questions.q: After CIRUS was permanently shut down (around 2010), which long-running BARC reactor remained the workhorse for radioisotope production and research?
questions.explanation: Dhruva became the principal high-flux reactor for isotope production and research after CIRUS was decommissioned.

questions.q: An upgraded version of India's first reactor, 'Apsara-U' (Apsara Upgraded), was made critical in which year?
questions.explanation: Apsara-U, a higher-power upgraded version of the original Apsara, attained criticality in 2018.

questions.q: Which nuclear scientist, who rose to head BARC and the Atomic Energy Commission, was a key figure in the 1998 Pokhran-II tests and championed the thorium programme?
questions.explanation: Anil Kakodkar, a former BARC director and AEC chairman, was central to Pokhran-II and India's thorium efforts.

questions.q: BARC's earliest small reactors included a heavy-water-moderated zero-energy reactor used for lattice studies. What was its name?
questions.explanation: Zerlina was a zero-energy, heavy-water reactor at Trombay used to study reactor lattice arrangements.

questions.q: BARC has also contributed to high-performance computing for nuclear simulations through a homegrown supercomputer series. What is that series called?
questions.explanation: BARC developed the Anupam series of parallel supercomputers for its scientific and nuclear computing needs.

questions.q: Which broad description best captures BARC's role within India's nuclear ecosystem?
options: Solely a commercial nuclear power plant operator
A multidisciplinary R&D centre spanning reactors, fuel cycle, isotopes and strategic programmes
A purely academic university granting nuclear degrees
An export agency selling reactors abroad
questions.explanation: BARC is a multidisciplinary research centre covering reactor design, fuel-cycle technology, isotopes, materials and strategic programmes, not a power-plant operator.
